content.body: A chemical reaction converts reactants into products with different properties. Chemical changes (unlike physical changes) are usually irreversible and involve making or breaking chemical bonds. Recognising types of reactions helps predict products and write equations.

Types of reactions:
1. SYNTHESIS: A + B → AB (two things combine)
   2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl

2. DECOMPOSITION: AB → A + B (one thing breaks apart)
   CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (thermal decomposition)

3. DISPLACEMENT: more reactive metal displaces less reactive
   Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu (Zn displaces Cu)

4. NEUTRALISATION: acid + base → salt + water
   HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O

5. COMBUSTION: fuel + oxygen → CO₂ + H₂O
   CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O

6. OXIDATION/REDUCTION: transfer of electrons (redox)

Signs of a chemical reaction:
• Colour change
• Gas produced (effervescence)
• Precipitate formed (solid forms in solution)
• Temperature change (heat given out or absorbed)
• Light emitted
• Smell produced
• Irreversible change

content.title: Writing and Balancing Chemical Equations
content.body:
Word equation: hydrogen + oxygen → water
Formula equation (unbalanced): H₂ + O₂ → H₂O
Balanced: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O

Rules:
1. Write correct formulas for all reactants and products
2. Balance by changing coefficients (big numbers in front)
3. NEVER change subscripts (small numbers in formula)
4. Check atoms of each element balance
5. Add state symbols: (s) solid (l) liquid (g) gas (aq) aqueous

questions.question: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ is an example of:
questions.explanation: Decomposition: one substance breaks down into two or more products. CaCO₃ decomposes when heated.

questions.question: Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu is:
questions.explanation: Zinc (more reactive) displaces copper from copper sulphate solution — a displacement reaction.

questions.question: Balancing: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O. Balanced equation?
questions.explanation: Left: 4H, 2O. Right: 4H, 2O. 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O is balanced.

questions.question: A precipitate is:
options: A gas formed in a reaction
A solid formed when two solutions react
questions.answer: A solid formed when two solutions react
questions.explanation: A precipitate is an insoluble solid that forms when two solutions react — it appears as a cloudy solid in solution.

questions.question: Which is NOT a sign of chemical reaction?
options: Change in state from liquid to gas by heating
questions.answer: Change in state from liquid to gas by heating
questions.explanation: Boiling is a physical change (no new substances formed). The others — colour change, gas, temperature change — indicate chemical reactions.

questions.question: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. State symbols: water is liquid, O₂ is gas. Write with symbols:
questions.explanation: H₂ is a gas (g), O₂ is a gas (g), liquid water is (l).

questions.question: Synthesis reaction example:
questions.explanation: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃: two reactants combine to form one product = synthesis.

questions.question: When balancing equations, you should change:
questions.explanation: Balance by changing the large numbers (coefficients) in front of formulas. NEVER change subscripts — that changes the substance.

questions.question: Fe + CuCl₂ → FeCl₂ + Cu. Which metal is more reactive?
questions.explanation: Iron displaces copper — iron is more reactive. More reactive metals displace less reactive ones from their compounds.

questions.question: (aq) state symbol means:
questions.explanation: (aq) = aqueous — the substance is dissolved in water to form a solution.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Metals are versatile materials used in construction (steel), electronics (copper, gold), jewellery (gold, silver), and transport (aluminium). The reactivity series ranks metals by their tendency to react — this determines how they are extracted from ores and how they corrode.

Reactivity Series (most reactive → least reactive):
Potassium (K)
Sodium (Na)
Calcium (Ca)
Magnesium (Mg)
Aluminium (Al)
Zinc (Zn)
Iron (Fe)
Lead (Pb)
Copper (Cu)
Silver (Ag)
Gold (Au)

Mnemonic: 'Please Stop Calling Me A Zebra In London, Can She Go'

Reactions with water and acid:
K, Na, Ca: react vigorously with COLD water → H₂ gas + metal hydroxide
2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂

Mg, Zn, Fe: react slowly with steam or dilute acid → H₂ gas
Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂

Cu, Ag, Au: NO reaction with water or dilute acid (too unreactive)

content.title: Extraction of Metals
content.body:
Very reactive metals (K, Na, Ca, Mg, Al): extracted by ELECTROLYSIS
(too reactive to be reduced by carbon)

Moderately reactive (Zn, Fe): extracted by REDUCTION WITH CARBON
Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂ (blast furnace)

Low reactivity (Cu, Ag, Au): found NATIVE (as pure metal in ground) or extracted by heating/simple reduction

Aluminium extraction (Hall-Héroult):
Al₂O₃ dissolved in molten cryolite → electrolysis → pure Al
Highly energy-intensive — recycling Al saves 95% of energy!

Corrosion:
Iron RUSTS when exposed to BOTH water AND oxygen:
4Fe + 3O₂ + 6H₂O → 4Fe(OH)₃ (iron hydroxide → rust)

Corrosion prevention:
• Painting (barrier)
• Oiling/greasing (barrier)
• Galvanising: coating with zinc (zinc sacrificed instead of iron — cathodic protection)
• Stainless steel alloy: Cr and Ni added to iron
• Electroplating with chromium or tin

Salt water accelerates rusting (electrolyte allows faster electron transfer)

questions.question: Which metal is most reactive?
questions.explanation: Potassium is at the top of the reactivity series — most reactive common metal.

questions.question: Sodium reacts with water. Product includes:
questions.explanation: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂↑. Sodium reacts vigorously with water, producing sodium hydroxide and hydrogen gas.

questions.question: Gold does not react with dilute acids because:
options: It is very unreactive (bottom of reactivity series)
questions.answer: It is very unreactive (bottom of reactivity series)
questions.explanation: Gold is at the bottom of the reactivity series — so unreactive it is found native (pure) in nature.

questions.question: Aluminium is extracted by electrolysis (not carbon reduction) because:
options: Aluminium is too reactive to be reduced by carbon
questions.answer: Aluminium is too reactive to be reduced by carbon
questions.explanation: Al is above carbon in the reactivity series. Carbon cannot displace it. Electrolysis is needed.

questions.question: Iron is extracted in a blast furnace by:
questions.explanation: Iron oxide (Fe₂O₃) is reduced by carbon monoxide (from coke) in the blast furnace: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂.

questions.question: Rusting requires:
questions.explanation: Iron rusts only when BOTH water and oxygen are present. Remove either and rusting stops.

questions.question: Galvanising protects iron by:
options: Coating with zinc, which is sacrificed instead of iron
questions.answer: Coating with zinc, which is sacrificed instead of iron
questions.explanation: Zinc is more reactive than iron. Even if scratched, zinc corrodes (sacrificial protection) while iron is protected.

questions.question: Can copper displace iron from iron sulphate solution?
options: No, iron is more reactive than copper
questions.answer: No, iron is more reactive than copper
questions.explanation: Only more reactive metals can displace less reactive ones. Iron is above copper in the reactivity series.

questions.question: Recycling aluminium saves energy because:
options: Re-melting needs only 5% of energy needed for electrolytic extraction
questions.answer: Re-melting needs only 5% of energy needed for electrolytic extraction
questions.explanation: Aluminium extraction by electrolysis uses enormous amounts of electricity. Re-melting scrap aluminium is 20× cheaper.

questions.question: Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + ? What gas is produced?
questions.explanation: Metals above hydrogen in reactivity series displace H₂ from dilute acids. Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂↑.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Oxidation and Reduction:
OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons)

Oxidation state rules:
• Uncombined element: 0
• O in compounds: −2 (except peroxides)
• H in compounds: +1 (except metal hydrides)
• Sum of oxidation states in compound = charge

content.title: Identifying Redox Reactions
content.body:
Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu
Zn: 0 → +2 (OXIDISED — loses 2e⁻)
Cu: +2 → 0 (REDUCED — gains 2e⁻)

Zinc is the REDUCING AGENT (causes reduction of copper, itself oxidised)
Copper ions are the OXIDISING AGENT

Any displacement reaction is a redox reaction

Reactivity series (most to least reactive):
K, Na, Li, Ca, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe, Sn, Pb, H, Cu, Ag, Au

A metal higher in the series displaces a lower one from its salt
Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu (Fe displaces Cu)
Cu + ZnSO₄ → No reaction (Cu less reactive than Zn)

questions.question: OIL RIG stands for...
options: Oxygen In Liquid — Reduction Is Gas
Oxidation Is Loss — Reduction Is Gain
Only Ionic Liquids React In Groups
questions.answer: Oxidation Is Loss — Reduction Is Gain
questions.explanation: OIL RIG mnemonic: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons).

questions.question: Oxidation state of O in H₂O is...
questions.explanation: Oxygen in compounds is almost always −2 (except in peroxides, where it's −1, and OF₂ where it's +2).

questions.question: In Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu, zinc is...
questions.explanation: Zn goes from 0 to +2 — it loses electrons (oxidised). It acts as the reducing agent.

questions.question: A reducing agent...
options: Causes oxidation and is itself oxidised
Causes reduction and is itself reduced
questions.answer: Causes oxidation and is itself oxidised
questions.explanation: A reducing agent donates electrons to another species (causing it to be reduced). The reducing agent itself loses electrons (is oxidised).

questions.question: Iron displaces copper from copper sulphate because...
options: Fe is more reactive than Cu in reactivity series
questions.answer: Fe is more reactive than Cu in reactivity series
questions.explanation: Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu. Iron is higher in the reactivity series → displaces copper.

questions.question: Will copper react with ZnSO₄ solution?
options: No reaction (Cu is less reactive than Zn)
questions.answer: No reaction (Cu is less reactive than Zn)
questions.explanation: A less reactive metal cannot displace a more reactive one. Cu < Zn in reactivity series → no reaction.

questions.question: What is the oxidation state of S in H₂SO₄?
Rusting of iron is a redox reaction. Iron is...
questions.explanation: Rusting: Fe → Fe²⁺ or Fe³⁺ (loses electrons) = oxidised. Fe acts as reducing agent; O₂ is the oxidising agent.

questions.question: In electrolysis, the cathode (negative electrode) causes...
questions.explanation: Cathode is negative → attracts positive ions and gives them electrons → REDUCTION occurs at cathode.

questions.question: Magnesium and hydrogen are in the reactivity series. Mg...
options: Reacts vigorously with HCl (Mg above H)
Is below H in the series
questions.answer: Reacts vigorously with HCl (Mg above H)
questions.explanation: Mg is above H in the reactivity series → Mg displaces H from HCl: Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂↑

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Chemical Reactions & Metals. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Chemical Reactions & Metals concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Chemical Reactions & Metals builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Chemical Reactions & Metals. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Chemical Reactions & Metals. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Chemical Reactions & Metals include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Chemical Reactions & Metals. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Chemical Reactions & Metals concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Chemical Reactions & Metals builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

Common examination questions on Chemical Reactions & Metals include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Chemical Reactions & Metals. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Chemical Reactions & Metals concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Chemical Reactions & Metals builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Chemical Reactions & Metals. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Chemical Reactions & Metals. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Chemical Reactions & Metals include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Chemical Reactions & Metals. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
